summary: Google announced it will ban payday loan advertisements through AdWords, requiring lenders to show APR of 36% or less and loan terms of at least 60 days. O'Neil sees this as a positive step but raises questions about enforcement, given payday lenders' history of finding loopholes around regulations. She notes that Google is effectively becoming a quasi-regulator, leapfrogging agencies like the CFPB. The move represents a significant judgment call beyond banning obviously illegal products, suggesting Google is beginning to acknowledge responsibility for vulnerable users in its advertising ecosystem.
key_points: Google's new policy bans payday loan ads with APR above 36% or terms under 60 days, but enforcement questions remain about lead aggregators and lender workarounds
Google is effectively acting as a quasi-regulator, moving faster than official bodies like the CFPB to protect consumers
The payday lending industry's 'free speech' objection is baseless since Google is a private platform, but the framing reveals how we might think about ad platforms as public spaces
Targeted advertising creates predatory 'back alleys' where vulnerable individuals are selected by zip code and search queries for exploitation
This represents a turning point where Google made a moral judgment call rather than just banning obviously illegal content
money_quotes: Google is leaping over regulators like the CFPB, which kind of makes Google into a quasi-regulator itself.
Imagine Google ads as a kind of corporate town square, where companies get to broadcast their wares to potential customers.

We should really imagine it as a series of back alleys in which the most vulnerable and desperate individuals, chosen just as much for their zip codes as for their search query, are taken aside and promised their problems will all go away if they just sign on the dotted line.
Instead of just getting rid of truly obvious bad actors (illegal drugs, counterfeit goods, explosives), Google has actually gone further and made a judgement call, and a good one.
They are starting to acknowledge the responsibility they have to the worst-off of the people who inhabit the environment they create.
Their argument is along the lines of calling it 'discriminatory and a form of censorship,' as if there's some freedom of speech issue here. There isn't, since Google is a private platform.
key_insight: When tech platforms make moral judgments about what ads to allow, they become de facto regulators with more power and speed than government agencies—a responsibility they're only beginning to acknowledge.
